name: sh (Python Subprocess Interface)

auth.notes: Local library; runs local system commands

performance.notes: Fork/exec overhead per command; equivalent to subprocess

agent_readiness.notes: Subprocess wrapper with shell-like ergonomics. import sh. sh.ls('-la'). sh.git('clone', 'https://...', '/tmp/repo'). sh.pip('install', 'requests'). result = sh.uname('-a'). str(result) — stdout. result.exit_code. Piping: sh.grep(sh.ls('/tmp'), 'file'). Streaming: for line in sh.tail('-f', '/var/log/app.log', _iter=True): print(line). Async: p = sh.long_process(_bg=True). p.wait(). Environment: sh.env = {'PATH': '/usr/bin'}. sh.Command('custom-tool')('-arg'). sh.contrib.git for git shortcuts. ErrorReturnCode exception on non-zero exit. Not available on Windows.

gotchas: Not available on Windows — Linux/macOS only
Shell injection risk if user input passed directly to commands — always pass as separate args
ErrorReturnCode raised on non-zero exit — must handle explicitly
No type annotations — limited IDE support
Importing system commands dynamically can be surprising (sh.doesnotexist raises AttributeError)
best_when: Scripting system commands with Python ergonomics (no shell=True)
Streaming output from long-running processes (tail -f equivalent)
Replacing subprocess.run() boilerplate with readable code
avoid_when: Windows environments — use subprocess or plumbum
Security-sensitive code with user-provided inputs (verify arg separation)
When subprocess.run() is sufficient (avoid adding dependency)
summary: sh wraps system commands as Python functions. import sh; sh.git('clone', url); sh.ls('-la'). Piping: sh.grep(sh.ls('/'), 'bin'). Streaming: _iter=True. Background: _bg=True. MIT by Andrew Moffat. Linux/macOS only. Best for readable subprocess scripting. Always pass args separately (not via shell string) to prevent injection. ErrorReturnCode on non-zero exit. No Windows support. No type annotations.
